concerns.text: The plan proposes a 'paired t-test on fold-wise errors' to compare the DFT-enhanced model against the composition-only baseline. This is methodologically unsound because the 5 folds are not independent samples; they share data points (each point is in 4 training sets and 1 test set). This violates the independence assumption of the t-test, inflating Type I error rates. A proper comparison requires either a nested cross-validation with a single hold-out test set for the final comparison, or a non-parametric test (e.g., Wilcoxon signed-rank) on the fold-wise errors with a correction for dependence, or a permutation test.

The power analysis is described as calculating power 'for the paired t-test' with a target n=20. However, the plan does not specify the expected effect size (Cohen's d) or the variance of the error difference between models. Without an estimated effect size derived from prior literature or a pilot study, the power calculation is circular or arbitrary. The plan must define how the effect size for the power analysis will be estimated (e.g., from a pilot run or literature) to ensure the sample size (n=20) is actually sufficient to detect the hypothesized improvement.

The Bootstrap Stability analysis resamples 'n=10 to n=50' with 10 iterations. Resampling down to n=10 for a Random Forest model is likely to result in high variance and unstable feature importance estimates that do not reflect the true stability of the model on the full dataset. This range (10-50) seems arbitrary and potentially misleading for assessing stability. The methodology should justify the lower bound (n=10) or focus on resampling the full dataset (n) with replacement to assess the variance of the estimator, rather than simulating a smaller dataset which introduces a different source of instability (sample size) rather than just estimator variance.
